headline: Mah Meri Cultural Village Receives Zero Government Heritage Funding
context: The Mah Meri Orang Asli cultural village on Carey Island, Selangor — preserving one of Malaysia's most distinctive indigenous art traditions — operates entirely on self-generated tourism revenue.

cards.big: Mah Meri woodcarving is internationally recognised as a masterwork art form.
cards.sub: The community's cultural village on Carey Island receives zero government heritage or cultural funding.

cards.big: Mah Meri population is approximately 3,800 people.
cards.sub: Fewer than 40 master carvers remain. Average age: 62. No apprenticeship programme is funded.

cards.big: Japan funds Living National Treasure programmes at JPY 2B annually.
cards.sub: Malaysia has no equivalent programme for indigenous master craftspeople.

cards.big: Carey Island development threatens the community's remaining 480 hectares.
cards.sub: Without land title security, the cultural village itself may be displaced by development.

cards.big: An internationally recognised art tradition with 40 ageing masters and zero funding. When they are gone, the art dies with them.
Indigenous art preservation requires living master programmes, apprenticeship funding, and land security. These cost less than a single highway interchange and preserve irreplaceable heritage.
